chapters.heading: Auto-timed | Preface
chapters.label: Preface
paragraphs: Most people still use AI like a search box with a better writing style. They ask one question, read one answer, and do the real work themselves.

An agent changes the unit of work. Instead of asking for advice, you delegate a longer task that may inspect files, use tools, make changes, check results, and keep going until it reaches a defined outcome.

OpenAI's 2026 Codex research shows the shift. In May, more than seventy percent of sampled users made at least one request estimated above an hour of human work, and one quarter made one above eight hours. But longer does not automatically mean suitable for delegation.
You will learn how to recognize an agent-ready task, place context and review around it, and choose among chat, copilot, supervised agent, and delegated workflow.
You will leave with a Task Upgrade Scorecard for research, documents, data, publishing, operations, and software work.

chapters.heading: Auto-timed | The Work Shift
chapters.label: The Work Shift
paragraphs: The first shift is simple: stop measuring AI work by the number of messages and start measuring the artifact or change produced.

The research distinguishes consultation from delegated production. Consultation gives you information or suggestions. Delegated production asks the system to create a document, transform data, modify a system, validate a change, or complete a workflow.

That difference appears across many tasks: drafting a memo from source files, cleaning a spreadsheet and checking totals, debugging a failing application, preparing a campaign, or producing structured research with citations.

Task horizon is evidence that users trust agents with larger pieces of work. By May 2026, eighty point six percent of sampled individual users had made a request estimated above thirty minutes of human work, seventy point two percent above one hour, and twenty-five point six percent above eight hours.

The broadest request also tends to arrive at the beginning of a thread. Later turns are narrower corrections and refinements. Good delegation therefore begins with a complete work packet, not with a vague sentence followed by twenty rounds of missing context.

Intensive users organize work differently. They run longer tasks, reuse skills, and sometimes coordinate several independent agents in parallel. The human role shifts from typing every step to defining work, monitoring progress, reviewing evidence, and deciding where to intervene.

Treat the numbers carefully. Human-time estimates are generated by a model and should be read as directional. OpenAI employees also work in an unusually favorable environment with broad access, low friction, and strong internal knowledge sharing, so their adoption pattern is not a promise for every team.
Chapter recap.
First, measure the artifact or change produced, not the number of messages.
Second, delegate a complete work packet rather than revealing essential context one correction at a time.
Third, treat task-horizon data as evidence of a shift, not as proof that every long task belongs to an agent.

chapters.heading: Auto-timed | Task Fit
chapters.label: Task Fit
paragraphs: A long task is not automatically a good agent task. The real question is whether the work has the structure an agent needs to make progress without inventing its own reality.

The first property is a concrete outcome. Ask for a finished artifact or an observable system change: a cleaned dataset, a tested patch, a reconciled report, or a publishing draft prepared for review.

Compare that with a request like, help with marketing. The topic is large, but success is invisible. A better task names the audience, source material, output format, constraints, and the decision the result must support.

The second property is accessible context and tools. The agent needs the real files, systems, references, and permissions required by the task. If critical context exists only in someone's memory or in an inaccessible application, the task is not ready for independent execution.

The third property is trustworthy feedback. A coding agent can run tests. A data task can reconcile totals and validate a schema. A browser task can verify that the page changed. A research task can attach citations and flag unsupported claims. Without feedback, an agent can continue moving while becoming less correct.

The fourth property is bounded risk. Reading, drafting, and preparing are reversible. Sending messages, spending money, deleting data, changing production, or publishing externally can create harm that is difficult to undo. Those actions need narrow permissions and explicit approval points.
When a task has a concrete outcome, accessible context, trustworthy feedback, and bounded risk, delegation becomes an operating choice rather than a leap of faith.
Chapter recap.
First, define a concrete artifact or observable change.
Second, provide the context, tools, and feedback needed to judge progress.
Third, bound the risk before you increase autonomy.

chapters.heading: Auto-timed | Upgrade Scorecard
chapters.label: Upgrade Scorecard
paragraphs: The Task Upgrade Scorecard turns those four properties into four practical gates.
Gate one asks whether the output is concrete. If you only need an explanation, comparison, or brainstorm, keep the task in chat. If you need a finished artifact, continue.

Gate two asks whether the work forms a contiguous action chain. The agent must be able to move from source material to action to result without repeatedly waiting for hidden information. If the chain is broken by unavailable systems or undocumented decisions, use AI as a copilot while the human performs the missing steps.

Gate three asks whether progress produces evidence. Each important step should return a signal that can change the plan: a test result, schema check, source citation, preview, reconciliation total, or provider response. If evidence exists but judgment is still difficult, use a supervised agent and add checkpoints.

Gate four asks whether permissions and consequences are bounded. Reversible work can continue automatically. Irreversible work should stop before the action and request approval. A capable agent does not need unlimited authority to be useful.

This scorecard is a practical synthesis of the research evidence, not a table copied from OpenAI. Its purpose is to translate the shift from conversation to delegation into a decision you can use today.

Consider a spreadsheet cleanup. The output is a corrected workbook, the inputs are available, formulas and totals provide feedback, and the original file can be preserved. That task can often reach supervised or delegated execution.

Now consider publishing a newsletter. Research, drafting, formatting, link checks, and preview generation can be delegated. The final external send is high impact, so the workflow should stop for approval even if every preparation step passes.

A research task fits when sources are accessible, claims remain linked to evidence, uncertainty is visible, and the deliverable has acceptance criteria. Asking an agent to decide a high-stakes policy from incomplete sources does not become safe merely because the task is long.

The four work modes are therefore simple. Use chat for answers. Use a copilot when the human still owns the action chain. Use a supervised agent when the chain works but checkpoints or judgment remain important. Use a delegated workflow when all four gates hold and the procedure is stable enough to repeat.
Chapter recap.
First, upgrade the work mode one gate at a time.
Second, keep irreversible actions behind explicit approval.
Third, choose the least autonomous mode that can reliably produce the required outcome.

chapters.heading: Auto-timed | Context and Feedback
chapters.label: Context and Feedback
paragraphs: Once a task passes the scorecard, the next job is to give the agent ground truth.

A useful context package contains five things: the goal, the current state, the relevant files or systems, the constraints, and the definition of done. More context is not automatically better. The useful context is the smallest set that lets the agent act without guessing.

Tool access should match the task. A document task may need a source folder and an output path. A data task may need a workbook plus validation rules. A publishing task may need an authenticated page, final copy, a thumbnail, and a clear instruction to stop before the publish button.
Then design the feedback loop. The agent acts, reads the real result, compares it with the acceptance criteria, and either continues, repairs, or stops.

This loop is what makes the path genuinely agentic. The plan can change from evidence, but it must not change from unsupported confidence. A test failure, missing source, invalid file, or provider rejection should become a new decision, not a detail to hide.

Long work also needs visible checkpoints. Save intermediate artifacts, record tool results, preserve errors, and make the current state recoverable. A human should be able to see what changed, what remains, and why the agent believes the task is complete.

This applies beyond software. A recruiting workflow can verify required fields and stop before outreach. A finance workflow can reconcile totals and surface exceptions. A content workflow can validate dimensions, chapters, and links before it asks for final publication approval.
Chapter recap.
First, package the smallest complete set of goal, state, context, constraints, and completion criteria.
Second, make every important action return evidence that can change the plan.
Third, preserve checkpoints so the work can be reviewed, resumed, and recovered.

chapters.heading: Auto-timed | Risk and Review
chapters.label: Risk and Review
paragraphs: Delegation does not remove human judgment. It changes where judgment creates the most value.

Low-risk actions such as reading, searching, drafting, and preparing can usually run with wider autonomy. High-impact actions such as sending, paying, deleting, changing production, or making a public claim should receive narrower permissions.

Place human approval immediately before an irreversible action, after the agent has prepared the evidence needed for a fast decision. Do not require approval for every harmless read, and do not wait until after a damaging action to review it.
Review capacity is also a real bottleneck. If an agent produces ten times more work but the output remains difficult to inspect, total productivity may not improve.

Design acceptance criteria before execution. Ask for a diff, a checklist, a reconciliation report, citations, test results, or a preview. The best review artifact makes important failures obvious without forcing the human to reconstruct the entire process.

Split the work where the risk changes. Research and drafting can be one automated stage. External sending can be a separate approval stage. Data transformation can run automatically while deletion of source records remains prohibited.
Add stopping conditions for missing context, repeated failure, budget limits, and ambiguous outcomes. An agent that knows when to stop is more useful than one that always claims to finish.
Chapter recap.
First, give wider autonomy to reversible preparation and narrower authority to irreversible action.
Second, define acceptance evidence before execution so review stays tractable.
Third, stop on missing context, repeated failure, or ambiguous completion.

chapters.heading: Auto-timed | Reusable Workflows
chapters.label: Reusable Workflows
paragraphs: The strongest agent users do not only delegate bigger tasks. They turn repeated work into reusable workflows.

OpenAI's research found growing use of skills and plugins that preserve instructions, tool access, and procedural context. In June 2026, about a quarter of active individual users and nearly a third of active organizational users invoked at least one skill.
Start by identifying a task that recurs with the same goal, inputs, constraints, and review standard.
Encode the procedure: where to find context, which tools to use, what order matters, which checks must pass, which actions are forbidden, and where approval is required.
Keep the workflow versioned and evidence-producing. When a failure occurs, update the procedure or tool interface instead of relying on the next agent to remember a conversation.

Parallel execution is valuable only when tasks are independent and the review burden remains manageable. Three agents can research separate markets or inspect separate modules. They should not all edit the same artifact without coordination and ownership rules.
The human role becomes workflow design and portfolio management: choose tasks, allocate context, watch blockers, review high-impact outputs, and improve the reusable system from real failures.
Chapter recap.
First, standardize tasks that repeat with the same inputs, constraints, and checks.
Second, reuse procedures that preserve context, tools, permissions, and evidence.
Third, parallelize independent work only when ownership and review remain clear.

chapters.heading: Auto-timed | Summary
chapters.label: Summary
paragraphs: Remember the complete method in three lines.
First, delegate work with a concrete output, not a vague topic.
Second, require accessible context and trustworthy feedback before the agent acts independently.
Third, bound permissions and put approval before irreversible actions.

The Task Upgrade Scorecard moves from chat to copilot, supervised agent, and delegated workflow only as those gates become true. The goal is not maximum autonomy. It is reliable production with less human handling and no loss of control.
Follow Tiny Agent for practical ways to choose AI tasks, package context, verify results, control risk, and turn one successful delegation into a repeatable workflow.
